title: HTTP Fundamentals
description: Understanding HTTP methods, status codes, headers, and the request-response lifecycle.

sections.heading: The Request-Response Cycle
sections.content:
Every interaction on the web follows a simple pattern: a client sends a request, and a server sends back a response. Understanding this cycle is the foundation of backend development.

A request consists of a method (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, etc.), a URL, headers, and optionally a body. The server processes the request and returns a response with a status code, headers, and a body.

sections.heading: HTTP Methods
sections.content:
The most common HTTP methods map to CRUD operations:

• GET — Read a resource. Should be idempotent and safe (no side effects).
• POST — Create a new resource. Not idempotent.
• PUT — Update/replace an entire resource. Idempotent.
• PATCH — Partially update a resource.
• DELETE — Remove a resource. Idempotent.

sections.heading: Status Codes
sections.content:
Status codes tell the client what happened:

• 1xx — Informational
• 2xx — Success (200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content)
• 3xx — Redirection (301 Moved Permanently, 304 Not Modified)
• 4xx — Client Error (400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found)
• 5xx — Server Error (500 Internal Server Error, 502 Bad Gateway, 503 Service Unavailable)

sections.heading: Headers
sections.content:
Headers carry metadata about the request or response:

• Content-Type — Describes the format of the body (application/json, text/html)
• Authorization — Carries authentication credentials
• Cache-Control — Directs caching behavior
• Accept — What content types the client can handle
• CORS headers — Control cross-origin access (Access-Control-Allow-Origin)

title: REST API Design
description: Designing clean, consistent, and scalable RESTful APIs with proper resource modeling.

sections.heading: REST Principles
sections.content:
REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style, not a protocol. Its core constraints are:

• Client-Server — Separation of concerns between UI and data storage.
• Stateless — Each request contains all information needed to process it.
• Cacheable — Responses must define themselves as cacheable or not.
• Uniform Interface — Consistent resource identification and manipulation.
• Layered System — Client doesn't know if it's connected directly to the server.

sections.heading: Resource Naming
sections.content:
Use nouns, not verbs. Resources are things, not actions.

• Good: /users, /orders/123, /users/5/orders
• Bad: /getUsers, /createOrder, /deleteUser

Use plural nouns for collections. Use path parameters for specific resources. Use query parameters for filtering, sorting, and pagination.

sections.heading: Response Design
sections.content:
Consistent response envelopes make APIs easier to consume:

• Always return JSON with a predictable structure.
• Include pagination metadata for list endpoints.
• Use appropriate status codes.
• Return the created/updated resource on write operations.

sections.heading: Versioning
sections.content:
APIs evolve. Versioning prevents breaking existing clients.

• URL versioning: /api/v1/users (most common, easy to understand)
• Header versioning: Accept: application/vnd.api+json;version=1
• Query parameter: /api/users?version=1

URL versioning is the simplest and most widely adopted approach.

title: Databases & Data Modeling
description: SQL vs NoSQL, schema design, indexing, queries, and data relationships.

sections.heading: SQL vs NoSQL
sections.content:
Choose based on your data and access patterns:

SQL (PostgreSQL, MySQL): Best for structured data with relationships, ACID compliance, complex queries, and strong consistency. Use when data integrity is critical.

NoSQL (MongoDB, Redis, DynamoDB): Best for flexible schemas, horizontal scaling, high write throughput, or document-oriented data. Use when speed and flexibility outweigh strict consistency.

sections.heading: Schema Design
sections.content:
Good schema design prevents performance problems later:

• Normalize to reduce redundancy (3NF for most applications).
• Denormalize strategically for read-heavy workloads.
• Choose appropriate data types — don't use VARCHAR(255) for everything.
• Add constraints at the database level (NOT NULL, UNIQUE, FOREIGN KEY).
• Use UUIDs vs auto-increment IDs based on your scaling needs.

sections.heading: Indexing
sections.content:
Indexes make reads fast but slow down writes:

• Index columns used in WHERE, JOIN, and ORDER BY clauses.
• Use composite indexes for multi-column queries (order matters).
• Avoid over-indexing — each index adds write overhead.
• Use EXPLAIN ANALYZE to verify your queries use indexes.
• Consider partial indexes for filtering subsets of data.

sections.heading: Migrations
sections.content:
Schema changes should be version-controlled and reversible:

• Use migration tools (Knex, Prisma, Flyway, Alembic).
• Each migration has an up (apply) and down (rollback) function.
• Never edit a migration that has been applied to production.
• Test migrations against production-like data volumes.
• Consider zero-downtime migration patterns for large tables.

title: Authentication & Authorization
description: JWT, OAuth 2.0, session management, role-based access control, and security best practices.

sections.heading: Authentication vs Authorization
sections.content:
Authentication answers: "Who are you?" — verifying identity.
Authorization answers: "What can you do?" — verifying permissions.

They are separate concerns and should be implemented as separate middleware layers.

sections.heading: Session-Based Auth
sections.content:
The traditional approach — server stores session state:

1. User submits credentials.
2. Server validates and creates a session (stored in memory, database, or Redis).
3. Server sends a session ID cookie.
4. Client sends the cookie on every subsequent request.
5. Server looks up the session to identify the user.

Pros: Easy to invalidate, server has full control.
Cons: Requires server-side storage, harder to scale horizontally.

sections.heading: Token-Based Auth (JWT)
sections.content:
Stateless approach — token contains the identity:

1. User submits credentials.
2. Server validates and issues a signed JWT.
3. Client stores the token and sends it in the Authorization header.
4. Server verifies the token signature — no database lookup needed.

Pros: Stateless, scales horizontally.
Cons: Can't easily revoke tokens, token size adds to every request.

sections.heading: Role-Based Access Control
sections.content:
RBAC assigns permissions to roles, and roles to users:

• Define roles: admin, editor, viewer
• Assign permissions to roles: admin can delete, editor can write, viewer can read
• Check permissions in middleware before executing the handler

title: Middleware & Request Pipeline
description: Building composable middleware for logging, validation, error handling, and more.

sections.heading: What Is Middleware?
sections.content:
Middleware functions sit between the request and the response. They can:

• Execute code before/after the handler
• Modify the request or response objects
• End the request-response cycle
• Call the next middleware in the stack

Think of middleware as a pipeline — each function processes the request and either passes it along or sends a response.

sections.heading: Common Middleware Patterns
sections.content:
Most backends use these middleware layers (in order):

1. Request logging — Log method, URL, and response time.
2. Body parsing — Parse JSON, URL-encoded, or multipart data.
3. CORS — Set cross-origin headers.
4. Authentication — Verify identity.
5. Rate limiting — Prevent abuse.
6. Route handlers — Business logic.
7. Error handling — Catch and format errors.

sections.heading: Validation Middleware
sections.content: Validate inputs before they reach your business logic. Never trust client data.

title: Error Handling & Logging
description: Structured error handling, logging strategies, and observability fundamentals.

sections.heading: Error Handling Strategy
sections.content:
A solid error handling strategy has three layers:

1. Operational errors — Expected failures (invalid input, network timeouts, auth failures). Handle gracefully with proper status codes and messages.

2. Programming errors — Bugs (null references, type errors). These should crash the process in development and be caught by a global handler in production.

3. Global catch-all — The last line of defense. Log the error, return a generic 500, and alert the team.

sections.heading: Custom Error Classes
sections.content: Create error classes that carry HTTP status codes and error codes:

sections.heading: Structured Logging
sections.content:
Log in JSON format for easy parsing and searching:

• Use log levels: error, warn, info, debug
• Include request IDs to trace requests across services
• Log at boundaries: incoming requests, outgoing calls, errors
• Never log sensitive data (passwords, tokens, PII)

title: Testing Backend Code
description: Unit tests, integration tests, API tests, and test-driven development for backend systems.

sections.heading: The Testing Pyramid
sections.content:
Structure your tests in layers:

• Unit tests (base) — Test individual functions and modules in isolation. Fast, many of these.
• Integration tests (middle) — Test how modules work together, including database queries. Moderate speed.
• End-to-end / API tests (top) — Test the full request-response cycle. Slow, fewer of these.

Aim for a healthy ratio: many unit tests, some integration tests, few E2E tests.

sections.heading: Unit Testing
sections.content: Test pure logic in isolation. Mock external dependencies.

sections.heading: API / Integration Testing
sections.content: Test the full HTTP layer with a real (test) database:

sections.heading: Test-Driven Development
sections.content:
TDD follows a simple cycle:

1. Red — Write a failing test for the behavior you want.
2. Green — Write the minimum code to make it pass.
3. Refactor — Clean up without changing behavior.

TDD works especially well for backend code because inputs and outputs are clearly defined (HTTP request in, JSON response out).

title: Security Best Practices
description: Input validation, SQL injection prevention, XSS, CSRF, rate limiting, and secure headers.

sections.heading: OWASP Top 10 for Backend Devs
sections.content:
The most critical security risks:

1. Injection — SQL, NoSQL, command injection. Always use parameterized queries.
2. Broken Authentication — Weak passwords, missing MFA, insecure token storage.
3. Sensitive Data Exposure — Unencrypted data, verbose errors leaking internals.
4. Broken Access Control — Missing authorization checks, IDOR vulnerabilities.
5. Security Misconfiguration — Default credentials, unnecessary services, verbose errors.
6. XSS — Even backend-rendered templates can inject unsanitized user input.
7. Insecure Deserialization — Never deserialize untrusted data.
8. Insufficient Logging — Can't detect attacks without proper audit logs.
9. SSRF — Server-side request forgery through unvalidated URLs.
10. Mass Assignment — Accepting all fields from user input into database models.

sections.heading: SQL Injection Prevention
sections.content: Never concatenate user input into SQL queries. Always use parameterized queries or an ORM.

sections.heading: Secure Headers
sections.content: Set security headers on every response:

sections.heading: Rate Limiting
sections.content: Protect your API from abuse and brute-force attacks:

title: Performance & Caching
description: Caching strategies, database optimization, async processing, and load testing.

sections.heading: Caching Layers
sections.content:
Cache at the right level for your use case:

• Application cache (in-memory) — Fastest, but limited to one process. Use for config, static lookups.
• Redis / Memcached — Shared cache across processes. Use for session data, API responses, computed results.
• HTTP cache — CDN/browser caching via Cache-Control headers. Use for static assets and rarely-changing API responses.
• Database query cache — Some databases cache query results. Understand your DB's caching behavior.

sections.heading: Redis Caching Pattern
sections.content: Cache-aside (lazy loading) is the most common pattern:

sections.heading: Database Optimization
sections.content:
Common performance wins:

• Use EXPLAIN ANALYZE on slow queries to understand the execution plan.
• Add indexes for frequently queried columns.
• Avoid SELECT * — fetch only the columns you need.
• Use connection pooling to avoid connection overhead.
• Batch inserts/updates instead of one-at-a-time.
• Consider read replicas for read-heavy workloads.

sections.heading: Async Processing
sections.content:
Move slow work out of the request path:

• Email sending — Queue it, don't block the response.
• Image processing — Offload to a worker.
• Report generation — Process in the background, notify when done.
• Third-party API calls — Queue if the response isn't needed immediately.

Use job queues (Bull, BullMQ, Celery, Sidekiq) to process work asynchronously with retries and monitoring.

title: Deployment & DevOps
description: CI/CD pipelines, Docker, environment configuration, health checks, and production readiness.

sections.heading: Environment Configuration
sections.content:
Never hardcode secrets or environment-specific values:

• Use environment variables for configuration.
• Use .env files for local development (never commit them).
• Use a secrets manager (AWS Secrets Manager, Vault) in production.
• Validate all required env vars at startup — fail fast with a clear error.

sections.heading: Docker
sections.content: Containerize your application for consistent deployments:
sections.code:
FROM node:20-alpine

WORKDIR /app

# Install dependencies first (cached layer)
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm ci --only=production

# Copy application code
COPY . .

EXPOSE 3000

HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=3s \
  CMD wget -qO- http://localhost:3000/health || exit 1

CMD ["node", "server.js"]

sections.heading: Health Checks
sections.content: Every production service needs health check endpoints:

sections.heading: CI/CD Pipeline
sections.content:
Automate your deployment pipeline:

1. Lint — Check code style and catch common errors.
2. Test — Run unit, integration, and API tests.
3. Build — Create the production artifact (Docker image).
4. Deploy to staging — Verify in a production-like environment.
5. Deploy to production — Use rolling deploys or blue-green.

Every merge to main should trigger this pipeline automatically. Never deploy manually to production.

title: Cram Session: .NET & Azure
description: Quick-hit review of Visual Studio, C#, ASP.NET MVC, Entity Framework, OData, Telerik/Kendo UI, WebForms, Azure DevOps, and Xamarin.

sections.heading: Visual Studio Experience
sections.content:
Visual Studio is the flagship IDE for .NET development. Key concepts to know:

• Solutions (.sln) contain one or more Projects (.csproj) — a solution is the top-level container.
• NuGet is the package manager (like npm for .NET). Packages are declared in the .csproj file.
• Built-in debugger: set breakpoints, inspect locals, watch expressions, and step through code (F5 to start, F10 step over, F11 step into).
• IntelliSense provides auto-completion, parameter info, and quick fixes.
• Server Explorer lets you connect to databases, Azure resources, and other services directly from the IDE.
• Use the Test Explorer window to run and debug unit tests (MSTest, NUnit, xUnit).

sections.heading: C# Fundamentals
sections.content:
C# is a statically-typed, object-oriented language on the .NET runtime. Key features:

• Strong typing with type inference via var — the compiler figures out the type at compile time.
• Async/await for non-blocking I/O — critical for web servers to handle concurrent requests.
• LINQ (Language Integrated Query) lets you query collections, databases, and XML with a consistent syntax.
• Properties are first-class citizens with get/set accessors.
• Generics, interfaces, and abstract classes for polymorphism.
• Nullable reference types (C# 8+) help eliminate null reference exceptions at compile time.

sections.heading: ASP.NET MVC Framework
sections.content:
ASP.NET MVC separates concerns into three components:

• Model — Data classes and business logic. Often Entity Framework entities or ViewModels.
• View — Razor templates (.cshtml) that render HTML with embedded C#.
• Controller — Handles HTTP requests, coordinates between Model and View.

The request pipeline: Routing → Controller → Action Method → View/JSON Result.

Key patterns:
• Attribute routing: [Route("api/[controller]")] on controller classes.
• Action filters: [Authorize], [ValidateAntiForgeryToken], custom filters.
• Dependency injection is built in — register services in Program.cs/Startup.cs.
• Model binding automatically maps request data (query, form, JSON body) to action parameters.

sections.heading: Entity Framework
sections.content:
Entity Framework (EF) is Microsoft's ORM for .NET. It maps C# classes to database tables.

• Code First: Define C# classes, EF generates the database schema via migrations.
• DbContext is the main class — it represents a session with the database and exposes DbSet<T> for each table.
• Migrations: Run dotnet ef migrations add <Name> to generate, dotnet ef database update to apply.
• Lazy loading vs Eager loading: Use .Include() for eager loading to avoid N+1 query problems.
• Change tracking: EF tracks modifications to entities and generates UPDATE statements on SaveChanges().
• EF Core is the modern, cross-platform version (EF6 is the legacy .NET Framework version).

sections.heading: OData
sections.content:
OData (Open Data Protocol) is a standard for building queryable REST APIs. It lets clients control what data they get back.

• $select — Choose which fields to return: /api/products?$select=Name,Price
• $filter — Server-side filtering: /api/products?$filter=Price gt 50
• $orderby — Sorting: /api/products?$orderby=Price desc
• $expand — Include related entities (like SQL JOIN): /api/orders?$expand=Customer
• $top / $skip — Pagination: /api/products?$top=10&$skip=20
• $count — Return total count: /api/products?$count=true

OData removes the need to write custom query endpoints for every filtering/sorting combination. The client describes what it needs, and the server translates it to efficient queries.

sections.heading: Telerik UI & Kendo UI
sections.content:
Telerik UI and Kendo UI are commercial UI component libraries by Progress Software.

• Kendo UI is the core JavaScript framework — works with jQuery, Angular, React, and Vue.
• Telerik UI for ASP.NET MVC / Core wraps Kendo UI widgets with server-side Razor helpers.

Key components frequently used in enterprise apps:
• Grid — Feature-rich data table with sorting, filtering, paging, grouping, and inline editing. Supports server-side operations for large datasets.
• Scheduler — Calendar/event scheduling component.
• Charts — Bar, line, pie, and dozens of other chart types.
• DropDownList / ComboBox / MultiSelect — Enhanced selection controls with search and templates.
• Window / Dialog — Modal and non-modal popup containers.

The Grid is the most important component — it can bind to OData endpoints for automatic server-side filtering, sorting, and paging.

sections.heading: WebForms
sections.content:
ASP.NET WebForms is the legacy event-driven model for building web apps. It's important to understand because many enterprise apps still use it.

• Pages are .aspx files with code-behind (.aspx.cs) — similar to Windows Forms but for the web.
• Server controls (asp:GridView, asp:Button, asp:TextBox) render HTML and manage state.
• ViewState: A hidden form field that persists control state between postbacks. Can bloat page size.
• Page lifecycle: Init → Load → Postback event handling → Render → Unload.
• Postbacks: User interactions trigger full page round-trips to the server (UpdatePanel enables partial/async postbacks).

Modern guidance is to use MVC or Razor Pages for new projects, but knowing WebForms is essential for maintaining legacy applications.

Key differences from MVC:
• WebForms abstracts away HTTP — you work with events, not requests/responses.
• Tight coupling between UI and logic (no clean separation of concerns).
• Testing is harder because of the page lifecycle and ViewState dependencies.

sections.heading: Azure DevOps
sections.content:
Azure DevOps is Microsoft's integrated platform for the full software development lifecycle.

• Azure Boards — Work item tracking (Epics, Features, User Stories, Tasks, Bugs). Supports Scrum, Kanban, and Agile process templates.
• Azure Repos — Git repositories with branch policies, pull request reviews, and merge strategies (squash, rebase, merge commit).
• Azure Pipelines — CI/CD automation. Define build and release pipelines in YAML or the visual designer.
• Azure Test Plans — Manual and automated test management.
• Azure Artifacts — Package feeds for NuGet, npm, Maven, and Python packages.

Key pipeline concepts:
• Triggers define when pipelines run (push to branch, PR, schedule).
• Stages → Jobs → Steps: the hierarchy of pipeline work.
• Service connections link to external resources (Azure subscriptions, Docker registries, Kubernetes clusters).
• Variable groups store shared configuration across pipelines.
sections.code:
# azure-pipelines.yml — basic .NET build + test
trigger:
  branches:
    include:
      - main
      - develop

pool:
  vmImage: 'ubuntu-latest'

variables:
  buildConfiguration: 'Release'

steps:
  - task: UseDotNet@2
    inputs:
      packageType: 'sdk'
      version: '8.0.x'

  - script: dotnet restore
    displayName: 'Restore NuGet packages'

  - script: dotnet build --configuration $(buildConfiguration)
    displayName: 'Build'

  - script: dotnet test --configuration $(buildConfiguration) --logger trx
    displayName: 'Run tests'

  - task: PublishTestResults@2
    inputs:
      testResultsFormat: 'VSTest'
      testResultsFiles: '**/*.trx'

sections.heading: Xamarin
sections.content:
Xamarin enables cross-platform mobile development using C# and .NET.

• Xamarin.Forms (now .NET MAUI) — Write one UI in XAML that renders native controls on iOS, Android, and Windows.
• Xamarin.iOS / Xamarin.Android — Platform-specific APIs when you need full native access.
• Shared code: Business logic, data access, and networking live in a shared project. Only platform-specific UI or features need separate code.
• XAML defines the UI declaratively with data binding to ViewModels (MVVM pattern).
• Dependency injection via constructor injection, just like ASP.NET Core.

Key architecture pattern — MVVM:
• Model — Data and business logic.
• View — XAML pages and controls.
• ViewModel — Exposes data and commands for the View, implements INotifyPropertyChanged.

Note: Xamarin.Forms has been succeeded by .NET MAUI (Multi-platform App UI) as of .NET 6+. MAUI uses the same concepts but with a single project structure and improved hot reload.
